# RedwoodSDK: Custom 404 & ErrorResponse Handling ## Background A RedwoodSDK project is pre-installed at `/home/user/myapp`. Implement a custom 404 page (rendered as React JSX) and demonstrate `ErrorResponse` short-circuiting from `rwsdk/worker`. ## Requirements Implement the routes below. The 404 page must be rendered through React (JSX). The `ErrorResponse` route demonstrates throwing an `ErrorResponse` instance. ## Acceptance Criteria * Project path: /home/user/myapp * Start command: npm run dev -- --host 0.0.0.0 --port 5173 * Port: 5173 * Routes: * `GET /home` returns JSX whose HTML contains the text `Welcome home`. * Any unmatched URL (e.g. `/does-not-exist`, `/nope/whatever`) must respond with status 404 and HTML containing `

Page Not Found

` and the text `The page you requested could not be found.`. * `GET /boom` must throw `new ErrorResponse(418, "Short and stout")` from inside its handler. The framework's default behaviour must surface a 418 response. The response body must contain the substring `Short and stout`. * `GET /healthcheck` returns `Response` with body `ok` and status 200.

Let me analyze the requirements: 1. I need to implement routes in a RedwoodSDK project at `/home/user/myapp` 2. Routes needed: - `GET /home` - returns JSX with "Welcome home" - Any unmatched URL - 404 page with `

Page Not Found

` and `The page you requested could not be found.` - `GET /boom` - throws `new ErrorResponse(418, "Short and stout")` from `rwsdk/worker` - `GET /healthcheck` - returns `Response` with body `ok` and status 200 Let me first explore the project structure to understand how RedwoodSDK works.

Implement GET /home route returning JSX with 'Welcome home'

Implement custom 404 page with

Page Not Found

and 'The page you requested could not be found.'
Implement GET /boom route throwing ErrorResponse(418, 'Short and stout')
Implement GET /healthcheck route returning 'ok' with status 200
Start dev server and test all routes

{
  "name": "@redwoodjs/starter",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "A bare-bones RedwoodSDK starter",
  "main": "index.js",
  "type": "module",
  "keywords": [],
  "author": "",
  "license": "MIT",
  "private": true,
  "scripts": {
    "build": "vite build",
    "dev": "vite dev",
    "dev:init": "rw-scripts dev-init",
    "preview": "vite preview",
    "worker:run": "rw-scripts worker-run",
    "clean": "npm run clean:vite",
    "clean:vite": "rm -rf ./node_modules/.vite",
    "release": "rw-scripts ensure-deploy-env && npm run clean && npm run build && wrangler deploy",
    "generate": "rw-scripts ensure-env && wrangler types --include-runtime false",
    "check": "npm run generate && npm run types",
    "types": "tsc"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "react": "19.2.6",
    "react-dom": "19.2.6",
    "react-server-dom-webpack": "19.2.6",
    "rwsdk": "1.2.11"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@cloudflare/vite-plugin": "1.33.2",
    "@cloudflare/workers-types": "4.20260426.1",
    "@types/node": "~25.6.0",
    "@types/react": "19.2.14",
    "@types/react-dom": "19.2.3",
    "typescript": "6.0.3",
    "vite": "~7.3.2",
    "wrangler": "4.85.0"
  },
  "pnpm": {
    "onlyBuiltDependencies": [
      "esbuild",
      "sharp",
      "workerd"
    ]
  }
}

export const Document: React.FC<{ children: React.ReactNode }> = ({
  children,
}) => (
  <html lang="en">
    <head>
      <meta charSet="utf-8" />
      <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
      <title>@redwoodjs/starter-minimal</title>
      <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
      <link
        rel="preconnect"
        href="https://fonts.gstatic.com"
        crossOrigin=""
      />
      <link
        rel="stylesheet"
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Noto+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,400..900;1,400..900&display=optional"
        precedence="first"
      />
      <link rel="modulepreload" href="/src/client.tsx" />
    </head>
    <body>
      {children}
      <script>import("/src/client.tsx")</script>
    </body>
  </html>
);

part.text:
So by default, when no routes match, the framework returns a plain text "Not Found" with status 404. I need to understand how to customize this to render a React JSX 404 page.

Let me look at the router source more closely to understand how 404 is handled and how I can override it.

I see that when no route matches, the framework returns `new Response("Not Found", { status: 404 })`. To customize this, I need to use a wildcard route (`/*`) or find another way to handle 404s.

Let me also look at the `except` handler and how the `ErrorResponse` works, since the requirement says to throw `new ErrorResponse(418, "Short and stout")` from `rwsdk/worker`.

Let me look at the test file for more context on how `except` works and how 404 handling isdone.

part.text: Let me look at the `except` test cases more closely, specifically how it handles ErrorResponse:
The `ErrorResponse` class is not in the router.js. Let me look at the error module and also understand how ErrorResponse is handled when thrown. Let me look at the actual runtime code.
Let me look at the worker.js to understand how ErrorResponse is handled when thrown.

Now I understand how `ErrorResponse` works. When an `ErrorResponse` is thrown: 1. If there's an `except` handler that catches it, the except handler can handle it 2. If it bubbles up to the top-level error handler in `defineApp`, it creates a `new Response(e.message, { status: e.code })` - so for `new ErrorResponse(418, "Short and stout")`, it would return a response with status 418 and body "Short and stout". Now I need to figure out how to handle 404 pages. Looking at the code, when no route matches, the framework returns `new Response("Not Found", { status: 404 })` directly, not through the `except` handler. So I need to use a wildcard route to catch all unmatched paths. Let me also look at how the `except` handler works with JSX (React) - this is important for the 404 page. Looking at the test cases, I can see that `except` handlers can return JSX elements. And looking at the test: ``` it("should return JSX element from except handler", async () => { ``` So the `except` handler can return a React component for error handling. Now let me plan my implementation: 1. **`GET /home`** - Add a route for `/home` that returns JSX with "Welcome home" 2. **Custom 404 page** - Use a wildcard route `/*` or an `except` handler to catch unmatched routes and render a 404 page with `

Page Not Found

` and "The page you requested could not be found." 3. **`GET /boom`** - Add a route that throws `new ErrorResponse(418, "Short and stout")` from `rwsdk/worker` 4. **`GET /healthcheck`** - Add a route that returns a plain `Response` with body "ok" and status 200 For the 404 page, I need to think about this carefully. The `except` handler catches errors thrown during request handling, but the built-in 404 ("Not Found") happens when no route matches. I have two approaches: 1. Use a wildcard route `/*` at the end to catch all unmatched routes and render the 404 page 2. Use the `except` handler approach Let me look at how the wildcard route works in the test: ``` route("*", WildcardPage) ``` This matches all paths. So I can add this at the end of my route definitions. But wait - I need to be careful. The current setup uses `render(Document, [...])` which wraps routes with a Document. For the 404 page to render as JSX (React), it should also be within a render block. Let me think about the structure: ```tsx export default defineApp([ setCommonHeaders(), ({ ctx }) => { ctx; }, render(Document, [ route("/home", Home), route("/boom", () => { throw new ErrorResponse(418, "Short and stout"); }), route("/healthcheck", () => new Response("ok", { status: 200 })), route("/*", NotFound), // catch-all for 404 ]), ]); ``` Wait, but the `/boom` route throws `ErrorResponse`. If I use an `except` handler, it might catch that error before the framework's default handling. But I want the framework's default behavior to handle it (which creates a `new Response(e.message, { status: e.code })`). Actually, looking more carefully at the code, the `except` handler would intercept the `ErrorResponse` before it reaches the top-level handler in `defineApp`. So if I add an `except` handler for 404, I need to make sure it doesn't interfere with the `/boom` route's `ErrorResponse`. Actually, let me re-read the requirement: - `GET /boom` must throw `new ErrorResponse(418, "Short and stout")` from inside its handler. The framework's default behaviour must surface a 418 response. The response body must contain the substring `Short and stout`. So I should NOT use an `except` handler that would catch ErrorResponse, because the requirement says the framework's default behavior must handle it. The framework's default behavior creates `new Response(e.message, { status: e.code })`, which will have status 418 and body "Short and stout" - exactly what we want. For the 404 page, I should use a wildcard route approach. Let me look at how wildcard routes work: - `route("/*", NotFoundPage)` - this should catch all unmatched routes But wait, there's a subtlety. The wildcard route would match ALL paths, including `/home`, `/boom`, and `/healthcheck`. Routes are matched in order, so I need to put the wildcard route LAST so that more specific routes match first. Let me also check: when using `render(Document, [...])`, does the 404 page get rendered within the Document component? Yes, because it's a route within the render block. Now let me also check: the current setup has `route("/", Home)`. I need to change this to `route("/home", Home)` based on the requirements. Let me also think about whether the `/healthcheck` route should be inside or outside the `render` block. Since it returns a plain `Response` object (not JSX), it can be inside the render block - the framework handles both JSX and Response returns from route handlers. Actually, let me reconsider. The `/healthcheck` route should return a plain Response with body "ok" and status 200. If it's inside the render block, it might get wrapped in the Document component. Let me check how that works... Looking at the router code, when a route handler returns a `Response` object, it short-circuits and returns that Response directly without rendering through the Document. So it's fine to put it inside the render block. Wait, actually let me check this more carefully. Let me look at the router.js to see how Response returns are handled.
Let me read the relevant parts of the router to understand how Response objects are handled inroute handlers.

I can see that when a route handler returns a `Response` object, it's returned directly without rendering through the Document. This is what I need for the `/healthcheck` endpoint. Now let me also understand how the `except` handler works with JSX. Looking at the test, it seems like `except` handlers can return JSX elements, and those elements would be rendered through the Document. Now let me also check: for the 404 page, should I use a wildcard route or an `except` handler? The requirement says "Any unmatched URL must respond with status 404 and HTML containing `

Page Not Found

` and the text `The page you requested could not be found.`" The key here is that the 404 page must: 1. Have status 404 2. Contain HTML with `

Page Not Found

` and `The page you requested could not be found.` If I use a wildcard route, the matched route would normally return a 200 status. I need to explicitly set the status to 404. Actually, looking at this more carefully, I think I need to use a combination approach. Let me think again... Option 1: Use a wildcard route that renders a 404 page with status 404 - Problem: Wildcard routes in `render` blocks render JSX within the Document, but I need to set the status to 404. Option 2: Use an `except` handler that catches the default 404 error - Problem: The default 404 is returned as a plain Response, not thrown as an error. So `except` won't catch it. Wait, let me re-read the router code. When no route matches, it returns `new Response("Not Found", { status: 404 })` directly. This is not an error that `except` would catch. So I need to use a wildcard route approach. Let me figure out how to set the response status to 404 when rendering JSX. Looking at the middleware pattern, I can set the status in a route handler. Let me check the `requestInfo` or `response` object. Looking at the `setCommonHeaders` middleware in the existing code, it has access to `response` headers. Let me look at how to set the status code. Actually, looking at the RouteMiddleware type: ```typescript export type RouteMiddleware = BivariantRouteHandler>; ``` And the RequestInfo type should have a `response` property. Let me check.

I see that `response` in `RequestInfo` is `ResponseInit & { headers: Headers }`. Let me check what `ResponseInit` includes:

`ResponseInit` from the Web API includes `status`, `statusText`, and `headers`. So I can set `requestInfo.response.status = 404` in the route handler.

So for the 404 page, I can create a component that sets the response status to 404 and renders the 404 page JSX.

Now let me also check the RwContext type:

Now I have all the information I need. Let me plan the implementation: 1. **`GET /home`** - Route that returns JSX with "Welcome home" 2. **Custom 404 page** - A wildcard route `/*` that: - Sets `response.status = 404` - Returns JSX with `

Page Not Found

` and `The page you requested could not be found.` 3. **`GET /boom`** - Route that throws `new ErrorResponse(418, "Short and stout")` 4. **`GET /healthcheck`** - Route that returns `new Response("ok", { status: 200 })` Now let me implement this. I need to modify: 1. `src/worker.tsx` - Update the route definitions 2. Create a new file `src/app/pages/not-found.tsx` for the 404 page component 3. Modify `src/app/pages/home.tsx` to show "Welcome home" Let me also check the current `worker.tsx` structure again. The current setup uses `render(Document, [route("/", Home)])`. For the 404 page, I need to: - Create a NotFound component that renders JSX with the appropriate content - Use a wildcard route to catch all unmatched paths - Set the response status to 404 Let me also think about whether the `/healthcheck` route should be inside or outside the `render` block. Since it returns a plain `Response`, it can be inside the render block - the framework handles Response returns by returning them directly without rendering through the Document. However, there might be a concern about the `/healthcheck` route being inside a `render` block. If it's inside a `render` block, the Document component's headers (like CSP, etc.) would be set up, but since we're returning a plain Response directly, those headers wouldn't be applied. That's fine for a healthcheck endpoint. Actually, wait. Let me reconsider. If I put `/healthcheck` inside the render block, the setCommonHeaders middleware would run and set headers on the response object. But then the route handler returns a new Response("ok", { status: 200 }), which would bypass those headers. Let me verify... Looking at the router code, when a route handler returns a Response, it's returned directly. The `setCommonHeaders` middleware sets headers on `response.headers`, but when the handler returns a new Response, those headers aren't applied to the returned Response. So it should be fine. Actually, let me look more carefully at how the middleware and response headers work. The `setCommonHeaders` middleware modifies `response.headers` in the requestInfo. When the final Response is created by the framework, it merges these headers. But when a route handler returns its own Response object, that Response is returned as-is. Let me verify this by reading the worker.js morecarefully.
Now I have a clear understanding of the project structure and APIs. Let me implement the requiredroutes.
